perturbation.original_text: Each party receiving other confidential information ("Receiving Party") belonging to or received from the other party (the "Disclosing Party") shall safeguard the Disclosing Party's Other Confidential Information and shall not disclose it (or any concepts, inventions or other information derived there from) to third parties (unless required by any judicial or governmental request, requirement or order) or to use it (or any <PAGE> concepts, inventions or other information derived there from) for any purposes other than evaluation without the express prior written consent of the Disclosing Party set forth in a separate written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party receiving other confidential information ("Receiving Party") belonging to or received from the other party (the "Disclosing Party") shall safeguard the Disclosing Party's Other Confidential Information and shall not disclose it to third parties or use it for any purposes other than evaluation without the express prior written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the exception allowing disclosure of confidential information when required by judicial or governmental request, requirement, or order. This introduces a legal contradiction because parties are legally obligated to comply with valid court orders and subpoenas, even if those orders require disclosure of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires compliance with subpoenas and court orders. The original clause acknowledged this obligation, while the modified version implies that confidential information is protected even in the face of a valid legal demand, which is false and creates legal risk for any party attempting to enforce the modified clause.

MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 3. OF THE PRODUCTION OF EVIDENCE [1985 - 2015.6] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Means of Production [1985 - 1997] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) 1987. (a) Except as provided in Sections 68097.1 to 68097.8, inclusive, of the Government Code, the service of a subpoena is made by delivering a copy, or a ticket containing its substance, to the witness personally, giving or offering to the witness at the same time, if demanded by him or her, the fees to which he or she is entitled for travel to and from the place designated, and one day’s attendance there. The service shall be made so as to allow the witness a reasonable time for preparation and travel to the place of attendance. The service may be made by any person. If service is to be made on a minor, service shall be made on the minor’s parent, guardian, conservator, or similar fiduciary, or if one of those persons cannot be located with reasonable diligence, service shall be made on any person having the care or control of the minor or with whom the minor resides or by whom the minor is employed, and on the minor if the minor is 12 years of age or older. If the minor is alleged
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the exception for disclosing confidential information when required by a judicial or governmental request, requirement, or order. This directly contradicts the legal obligation to comply with valid court orders and subpoenas, as parties are legally obligated to comply with valid court orders and subpoenas, even if those orders require disclosure of confidential information. Snippet 1 states, '...the service of a subpoena is made by delivering a copy...to the witness personally...' This implies a legal obligation to comply with subpoenas, which the modified text undermines by removing the exception for disclosure under such orders.

perturbation.original_text: The disclosing Party shall identify the written Other Confidential Information or any other Confidential Information disclosed in tangible media by marketing it "Confidential" or "Proprietary" or "Secret" or words of similar import. Other Confidential Information disclosed orally shall be thereafter summarized by the Disclosing party and the summary provided to the Receiving Party clearly identifying that such information is other party that was not known by such party or commonly known in the public domain shall be presumed to be other confidential for purposes of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The disclosing Party may identify the written Other Confidential Information or any other Confidential Information disclosed in tangible media. Other Confidential Information disclosed orally shall be summarized by the Disclosing party and provided to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall' to 'may', the obligation to identify confidential information is removed. Furthermore, removing the clarifying sentence regarding information already known or in the public domain eliminates important context. This weakens the protection of confidential information. Trade secret laws often require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Eliminating the mandatory labeling requirement makes it harder to prove that reasonable efforts were taken.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires a showing of reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. This change removes a mandatory process for marking confidential information, making it more difficult to prove that 'reasonable efforts' were made to protect the trade secret, potentially invalidating trade secret claims.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the disclosing party to identify confidential information, whereas the changed text makes it optional. The Law Explanation states that the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires demonstrating reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The Perturbation Explanation elaborates that removing the mandatory labeling makes it more difficult to prove such efforts. Snippet 1 includes 'This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act,' confirming the law's relevance. By removing the 'shall' obligation, the modified contract weakens the protection of trade secrets, making it harder to prove 'reasonable efforts' were taken, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall restrict access to the other party's other confidential information (and any concepts, invention or other information derived there from) to only those of its employees, representatives or advisors to whom such access is reasonably necessary or appropriate for carrying out the permitted use of such other confidential information. Each party shall to obtain Non-Disclosure Agreements containing appropriate restrictions on the persons signing the same prior to disclosing other confidential information (or any concepts, inventions or other information derived there from) to such persons.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall restrict access to the other party's other confidential information to its employees, representatives or advisors. Each party may obtain Non-Disclosure Agreements from persons prior to disclosing other confidential information to such persons.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the 'reasonably necessary or appropriate' qualifier and changes 'shall' to 'may' regarding NDAs. Limiting access only to individuals who *need* the information is a key element of reasonable secrecy. Removing this and making NDAs optional significantly weakens the protection. Making NDAs optional conflicts with best practices for trade secret protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires 'efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy'. Removing the 'need to know' limitation on access and removing the mandatory NDA requirement weaken secrecy, making it harder to prove reasonable efforts were taken.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens trade secret protection by removing the 'reasonably necessary or appropriate' qualifier for access to confidential information and making NDAs optional. This contradicts the principle outlined in the Law Explanation, which states that the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires 'efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy'. Removing these safeguards makes it more difficult to demonstrate that reasonable efforts were taken to maintain secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code 3426, which refers to the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. While this snippet doesn't directly define 'reasonable efforts', it establishes the Act's applicability, making the changes a significant weakening of required protections.

perturbation.original_text: The parties acknowledge that the breach of threatened breach of this Agreement may result in irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party and that, in addition to its other remedies, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to injunctive relief to restrain any threatened or continued breach of this Agreement. The parties hereby waive any requirement for the posting of a 2 <PAGE> bond or other security in connection with the granting to the Disclosing Party of such injunctive relief.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties acknowledge that the breach of this Agreement may result in injury to the Disclosing Party and that, in addition to its other remedies, the Disclosing Party may be entitled to injunctive relief to restrain any breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This removes 'irreparable' before injury and removes the waiver of bond. To obtain injunctive relief, a party must demonstrate irreparable harm. By removing 'irreparable', the agreement acknowledges only general 'injury,' making it harder to obtain an injunction. Furthermore, waiving the bond requirement makes it easier and faster to obtain injunctive relief. Removing this waiver makes it more difficult and costly to obtain an injunction.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law governs the granting of injunctions. To receive an injunction, 'irreparable harm' must be demonstrated absent an agreement to the contrary. Further, the requirement of a bond is standard practice and a way to compensate the enjoined party if the injunction is later deemed to be wrongly issued. By removing the explicit acknowledgement of 'irreparable injury' and the bond waiver, the contract makes it more difficult and costly to get an injunction, contradicting established legal precedent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 526 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 7. OTHER PROVISIONAL REMEDIES IN CIVIL ACTIONS [501 - 574] ( Heading of Title 7 added by Stats. 1974, Ch. 1516. ) CHAPTER 3. Injunction [525 - 534] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 526. (a) An injunction may be granted in the following cases: (1) When it appears by the complaint that the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded, and the relief, or any part thereof, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of the act complained of, either for a limited period or perpetually. (2) When it appears by the complaint or affidavits that the commission or continuance of some act during the litigation would produce waste, or great or irreparable injury, to a party to the action. (3) When it appears, during the litigation, that a party to the action is doing, or threatens, or is about to do, or is procuring or suffering to be done, some act in violation of the rights of another party to the action respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual. (4) When pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief. (5) Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief. (6) Where the
perturbation.justification: The original text acknowledged "irreparable injury" which is crucial for obtaining injunctive relief. The modified text removes "irreparable," only acknowledging "injury." CCP 526(a)(2) states that an injunction may be granted when the commission of some act would produce "great or irreparable injury." By removing "irreparable," the changed text weakens the argument for injunctive relief, making it harder to obtain. Furthermore, the removal of the bond waiver makes it more difficult to obtain an injunction.
